subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and focused aim with explicit practical and theoretical relevance
Thorough description of the Working Memory Model and phonological loop
Precise operationalization of IV and DV in both null and alternative hypotheses
weaknesses: Minor terminology imprecision (âepisodic registerâ vs. âepisodic bufferâ)
Brief âso what?â discussion limits deeper contextualization
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this in band 5â6. The aim is clear and relevance explained, theory/model linked, and variables fully operationalized. Minor imprecision and brevity prevent the top mark, so a score of 5 is merited.

strengths: Research design choice is explained in terms of reducing demand characteristics and order effects
Sampling technique and participant choice are connected to study context
Controlled variables and materials are described with brief rationale
weaknesses: Lacks discussion of confounds like individual music preferences
Opportunity sampling biases are not addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance aligns with band 3â4: the design, sampling, and materials are explained rather than merely described. Depth is limited, so a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

strengths: Correctly chosen inferential (Mann-Whitney U) and descriptive statistics with accurate calculations
Graphical presentation (bar graph with error bars) is clear and hypothesis-linked
Findings are interpreted in relation to data and hypothesis
weaknesses: Use of means/SD for ordinal data is conceptually problematic
Lacks effect size or confidence intervals to deepen interpretation
Statistical write-up could be more transparently structured
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates appropriate and largely accurate application of statistics and graphing (band 5â6), but conceptual issues and missing depth warrant a band 3â4 score. A score of 4 balances strengths with key areas for improvement.

strengths: Findings are explicitly discussed with reference to the WMM and prior research
Strengths and limitations are relevant and linked to reliability, validity, and generalizability
Modifications are justified and explicitly linked to identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion could further contextualize effect size and Type II error risk
Some potential limitations (e.g., participantsâ musical training) are not acknowledged
Depth of critical reflection is competent but not exhaustive
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation shows clear linkage to theory, relevant strengths/limitations, and justified modifications (band 5â6). However, limited depth and omission of some critical points place it in band 3â4, justifying a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A clearly labelled bar graph with error bars directly addresses the hypothesis and depicts group means.  Axes, title and condition labels are correct.  Minor aesthetic issues (small font) but no substantive errors, thus band 5-6 with score 5.

Findings are explicitly interpreted: overlap of error bars, non-significant U result, acceptance of null hypothesis.  Interpretation is tied back to data and hypothesis.  Slightly brief discussion of effect size stops short of perfection; band 5-6, score 5, is justified.

Both IV (presence/absence of lo-fi music during rehearsal) and DV (number of monosyllabic words recalled) are clearly stated and fully operationalised.  Null and alternative hypotheses are presented using significant-difference language.  This meets all requirements for the top band; no omissions justify full 6.

Opportunity sampling is identified, and the choice of 13-17-year-old students is connected to the studyâs relevance (they often study with music).  This constitutes an explanation of both technique and participant choice, meeting band 3-4.  Limited discussion of sampling drawbacks keeps the mark to 3.

The Working Memory Model is outlined with its main components and the phonological loop is accurately described.  The student explicitly connects the Irrelevant Sound Effect and the expected disruption of rehearsal to the present investigation.  Description and linkage are both present and mostly accurate, matching band 5-6; some imprecision (e.g., calling the episodic buffer âepisodic registerâ) keeps the mark to 5 not 6.

The student contrasts results with WMM predictions and the Perham & Vizard study, offering plausible reasons (lo-fi softness, fewer conditions) for the discrepancy.  This moves beyond description into genuine discussion, fulfilling band 5-6.  Depth could be richer, hence 5.

The student states a clear aim â to investigate the effect of background (lo-fi) music on free recall â and explicitly explains why this is educationally relevant (optimising study environments for students).  The relevance is linked to both applied and theoretical value, satisfying the descriptor for band 5-6.  Minor brevity in the âso what?â discussion prevents awarding the full 6.

Three strengths and three limitations are stated and each is clearly explained in relation to reliability, validity, or generalisability, demonstrating relevance to the investigation.  Analysis is competent though not exhaustive; band 5-6, score 5.

Materials (PowerPoint, song, instructions) and several controlled variables (room, time, slide design) are listed with brief rationale, providing an explanation rather than mere description.  The depth is adequate but not exhaustive, so band 3-4, score 3, is appropriate.

Proposed modifications (larger, more diverse sample; more realistic task; earlier testing time) are explicitly linked to corresponding limitations (generalizability, ecological validity, participant fatigue) and briefly justified.  The linkage satisfies the top-band descriptor; limited methodological detail keeps mark to 5, not 6.

An independent-measures experiment is not only described but briefly justified in terms of reducing demand characteristics and order effects, which constitutes explanation.  However, depth is limited and possible weaknesses of the design are not addressed, so the strand fits the lower end of band 3-4 (score 3).

Descriptive (means, SDs) and inferential (two-tailed Mann-Whitney U) statistics are correctly chosen for an independent-measures design with non-parametric data.  Calculations, critical values, p-value and decision are presented accurately.  Minor conceptual slip (treating ordinal scores with means) prevents full 6 but performance clearly merits high band.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively outlines the Working Memory Modelâs components and focuses clearly on the phonological loop, providing strong theoretical context.
The term âEpisodic Registerâ is nonstandard; the buffer is conventionally called the âepisodic buffer.â Clarify terminology to align precisely with the WMM.
The description of Perham & Vizardâs serial recall task is clear, but free recall differs methodologically. Elaborate on this distinction to justify your task choice.
The investigation aim and its practical relevance for optimizing student study environments are articulated clearly and linked to theoretical implications.
The independent and dependent variables are precisely stated and operationalized, meeting topâband clarity for the hypothesis section.
Both null and alternative hypotheses are stated correctly with clear operational variables, demonstrating rigorous hypothesis formulation.
The independentâmeasures design is justified but lacks discussion of potential confounds like individual music preferences. Address these to strengthen design evaluation.
Consider acknowledging that an independent measures design reduces order effects but increases participant variability; discuss tradeâoffs explicitly.
Standardized instructions are described, but explain how they minimize procedural variability and enhance reliability.
Clarify why afternoon testing was chosen and how time-of-day effects were controlled or could influence recall.
Explain the rationale for using means/standard deviations on ordinal recall scores versus median/interquartile range for nonparametric data.
Using means and SDs for ordinal recall data is conceptually problematic; consider median and IQR or justify parametric summary.
The bar graph is clearly labelled with error bars and directly addresses the hypothesis in a visually effective way.
Discuss effect size (e.g., r or Cohenâs d) or confidence intervals to complement p-value interpretation and strengthen findings interpretation.
Graph interpretation is accurate, but linking to statistical power and potential Type II error risk would deepen the analysis.
The description of the Mann-Whitney U test result is confusing in phrasing; separate the critical value determination from the p-value report for clarity.
Structure the inferential write-up with discrete steps: test rationale, U calculation, critical value, p-value, decision, ensuring statistical transparency.
The discussion contrasts findings with the WMM and prior studies effectively; referencing effect size would further contextualize the nonâsignificant result.
Strengths are well identified and linked to reliability and validity, demonstrating strong self-evaluation.
Rehearsal duration and timing under music should be operationalized (e.g., length of song exposure) to ensure reproducibility.
Opportunity sampling is described but its biases (e.g., lack of representativeness) are not. Discuss these limitations.
Participant choice (13â17-year-old students) is well explained in terms of realâworld relevance for study tasks.
Choice of lofi background music is justified by its popularity in study contexts, demonstrating relevant material selection.
The PowerPoint formatting is mentioned twice; consolidate redundant sections (4 and 4.) to improve clarity and avoid duplication.
Controlled variables are comprehensively described and linked to reducing confounds, supporting experimental reliability.
Consider acknowledging participantsâ prior musical training as a limitation; such experience could modulate susceptibility to background music interference.
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and justified, reflecting a strong evaluation of methodological refinements.
Have you considered withinâsubjects counterbalancing as a modification to increase power and control individual differences?
